results.title: Pulsed microwave-enhanced efficient in-situ leaching of ion-adsorbed rare earth ores - ScienceDirect
excerpts:
During leaching, the microwave power was set to 119 W under a pulsed mode of 0.5 min on and 9.5 min off. This method achieves a rare earth leaching efficiency as high as 84.06%, representing an increase of 14.17% over conventional leaching, while also shortening the seepage cycle by 45.3%. Microwave effect increases the pore area, pore volume and bulk porosity of the ore body by 92.2%, 104.1% and 83.1%, respectively. Under the pulsed microwave mode with the specified on-off ratio, seepage is effectively enhanced without causing excessive migration of fine particles. The mechanism of microwave-enhanced leaching is revealed based on the microwave heating characteristics, the migration behavior of the leaching agent, and the evolution of mineral structure.
These findings demonstrate that rational regulation of microwave parameters can synergistically improve both rare earth leaching and reagent permeation, providing a theoretical foundation for more efficient _in-situ_ mining of ion-adsorbed rare earth ores.

## Graphical abstract

After pulsed microwave-column leaching, the pore network structure of the ion-adsorbed rare earth ores becomes more developed. The leaching efficiency of REEs reaches 84.06%, representing a 14.17% increase compared to conventional leaching, while the overall percolation cycle is significantly shortened by 45.3%. 1. [Download: Download high-res image (317KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1002072126000669-ga1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (317KB)")
2. [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S1002072126000669-ga1.jpg "Download full-size image")

## Introduction
Rare earth elements (REEs), known as the "vitamins of modern industry", play an irreplaceable role in strategic emerging industries [1], [2]. Due to their unique photoelectromagnetic properties, medium and heavy REEs have become indispensable key elements in high-tech fields such as new energy, national defense and military industry, and artificial intelligence [3], [4]. As the source of 95% of the world's heavy REEs, the efficient development and utilization of ion-adsorption type rare earth ores have attracted widespread attention [5]. These ores primarily occur in weathered crust strata in southern China, where REEs are adsorbed in ionic form onto clay mineral surfaces and can be extracted via cation exchange [6], [7]. Currently, _in-situ_ leaching is the main method for mining such ores. However, this process faces challenges such as poor ore permeability and long leaching cycles.

results.title: Generation of Acoustic Signals by Pulsed Microwave Energy ...
excerpts: The phenomenon of sound generation from pulsed microwave energy was first noticed during preliminary experiments in 1969. These initial observations sparked further investigation into the mechanisms driving the 'hearing' of microwave signals.

results.title: Microwave auditory effect - Wikipedia
excerpts:
By changing transmitter parameters, Frey was able to induce the "perception of severe buffeting of the head, without such apparent vestibular symptoms as dizziness or nausea". Other transmitter parameters induced a pins and needles sensation. Frey experimented with nerve-deaf subjects, and speculated that the human detecting mechanism was in the cochlea , but at the time of the experiment the results were inconclusive due to factors such as tinnitus . <sup>[[ 1 ]]()</sup> <sup>[[ 5 ]]()</sup>

Auditory sensations of clicking or buzzing have been reported by some workers at modern-day microwave transmitting sites that emit pulsed microwave radiation. Auditory responses to transmitted frequencies from approximately 200 MHz to at least 3 GHz have been reported.
The cause is thought to be thermoelastic expansion of portions of auditory apparatus, and the generally accepted mechanism is rapid (but minuscule, in the range of 10 <sup>−5</sup> °C) heating of the brain by each pulse, and the resulting pressure wave traveling through the skull to the cochlea . <sup>[[ 5 ]]()</sup>

In 1975, an article by neuropsychologist Don Justesen discussing radiation effects on human perception referred to an experiment by Joseph C. Sharp and Mark Grove at the Walter Reed Army Institute of Research during which Sharp and Grove reportedly were able to recognize nine out of ten words transmitted by "voice modulated microwaves". Although it's been reported that "power levels required for transmitting sound... [would cause] brain damage due to... thermal effects", <sup>[[ 6 ]]()</sup> the pops associated with the microwave audio effect are not sustained over time, and the effect is due to brief, sudden increases in temperature.
<sup>[[ 7 ]]()</sup> In 1961, Frey reported the threshold levels for the microwave audio effect of 267 mW/cm² to be at 1.3 GHz and 5000 mW/cm² at 2.9 GHz for the peak amplitude (providing the pops), respectively. They would only give an average (sustained) power density of only about 0.4 mW/cm² and 2 mW/cm², respectively, <sup>[[ 7 ]]()</sup> similar to current cellphones. However, it's been argued that despite waves the microwave auditory effect only constituting a rapid 10 <sup>−6</sup> °C rise in temperature, for threshold peaks on each pulse, that, at the least, a strong peak of around 1400 kW/cm² (1.4 billion mW/cm²) would certainly be harmful due to the resulting pressure wave. <sup>[[ 8 ]]()</sup>

## Electronic warfare

In 2003–2004, WaveBand Corp. had a contract from the U.S. Navy for the design of an MAE system they called MEDUSA") (Mob Excess Deterrent Using Silent Audio) that was intended to temporarily incapacitate personnel through remote application.
